title: Introduction to Neural Networks
description: From the McCulloch-Pitts neuron to modern Transformers — a guided tour through the evolution of neural networks. Understand how each innovation built on the last.
details: Neural networks have cycled through waves of optimism, skepticism, and resurgence. Each breakthrough built on ideas from neuroscience, physics, mathematics, and engineering. The 2020s foundation model era represents the convergence of scale (data, compute, parameters) with algorithmic innovations (attention, normalization, residual connections) that the pioneers of the 1940s–1980s could only imagine. FAANG interviews probe this history to assess your grasp of first principles: why ReLU beats sigmoid, why skip connections enable 1000-layer nets, why attention replaces recurrence, and how gradient dynamics shape architecture design.

sections.title: 1943 — McCulloch-Pitts Neuron: The First Mathematical Neuron
sections.description:
Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts modelled a biological neuron as a binary threshold unit: inputs are binary (0/1), weights are fixed (no learning), and the output fires if the weighted sum exceeds a threshold θ. This could compute AND, OR, NOT — but not XOR.

Mathematical form:  y = 1 if Σ wᵢxᵢ ≥ θ, else 0

FAANG insight: The M-P neuron is the origin of every modern architecture. The core computation — linear transform + non-linear activation — is unchanged in transformers, CNNs, and LLMs. What changed: differentiability (step → smooth activations), learnable weights (backprop), scale (millions of compute units).

sections.title: 1958 — Rosenblatt's Perceptron: The First Learning Algorithm
sections.description:
Frank Rosenblatt introduced learnable weights. The perceptron update rule is stochastic gradient descent before SGD was formalized:

Perceptron Learning Rule:  w ← w + η(y − ŷ)x

The algorithm converges in finite steps for linearly separable data (Perceptron Convergence Theorem, 1962). But it fails on XOR — a single hyperplane cannot separate the corners of a square. This limitation halted funding for over a decade.

FAANG insight: The perceptron is a single-layer logistic regression with sign activation. The update rule is the same as SGD for the hinge loss (used in SVMs). Interviewers ask: why does the perceptron converge? (Answer: margin-based proof — the weight norm grows at most √(R²)/ρ where R = data radius, ρ = margin). The dual formulation (weight = weighted sum of misclassified points) foreshadows kernel methods and SVMs.

sections.title: Activation Functions & Weight Initialization
sections.description:
FAANG interviews frequently test activation functions: why ReLU dominates, when to use alternatives, and how initialization interacts with activation choice.

Common activations (most to least used today):
SwiGLU:  Swishβ(x)·W₂x       ← gated variant (LLaMA 1/2/3, PaLM, Gemma)
GELU:    x·Φ(x)              ← smooth ReLU (GPT-3/4, BERT, ViT)
ReLU:    max(0,x)              ← cheap, sparse, non-saturating (AlexNet, ResNet)
Sigmoid: σ(x)=1/(1+e⁻ˣ)     ← output in (0,1), saturates — avoid in hidden layers
Tanh:    (eˣ−e⁻ˣ)/(eˣ+e⁻ˣ) ← zero-centered, saturates — RNNs before LSTMs

Initialization (critical for trainability):
Xavier:  Var(w) = 2/(nᵢₙ + nᵢₒₙ)  ← for sigmoid/tanh, keeps gradient variance stable
He:      Var(w) = 2/nᵢₙ           ← for ReLU, accounts for 50% dead neurons

FAANG insight: ReLU’s advantage over sigmoid is avoiding saturation. Sigmoid derivative maxes at 0.25, so gradients shrink by 75%+ per layer (→ vanish at depth ~10). ReLU’s derivative is 0 or 1 — but 0 causes dead neurons (never activate). GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit) is the LLM default because its smooth curvature improves gradient flow vs ReLU’s hard kink. SwiGLU (LLaMA, PaLM) adds a learned gating Swishβ(x)·W₂x for richer expressivity at the cost of 50% more parameters.

Pop quiz: If all weights are initialized to zero, every neuron computes the same output, gradients are identical, symmetry never breaks — the network learns nothing. Always initialize with small random values.

[Intuition] Deep dive available: (→ see Activation Functions guide for formula, derivative, SVG curves, TF code, pros/cons, and FAANG insights for 10 activation functions including Swish, GELU, Mish, and Softplus)

sections.title: 1986 — Backpropagation: Chain Rule at Scale
sections.description:
Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986) showed that backpropagation — applying the chain rule recursively from output to input — trains multi-layer networks.

Chain rule for a single neuron:
L = ½(y − ŷ)²                    ← MSE loss
∂L/∂w = (∂L/∂ŷ) · (∂ŷ/∂z) · (∂z/∂w)    ← chain rule
  = −(y − ŷ) · σ′(z) · hᵢ                ← error × activation derivative × input

Hidden layer error (the key insight):
δᵢᵇᵈ = σ′(zᵢᵇᵈ) · Σⱼ wᵢⱼ · δⱼᵉᵒᵔ   ← error is weighted sum of downstream errors

FAANG insight: Backprop is the most common “code from scratch” interview question (e.g., implement a 2-layer MLP). Key points: (1) requires differentiable activations — step function fails (derivative = 0 almost everywhere), (2) vanishing gradients follow directly from the chain rule: if σ′(z) ≈ 0 (saturated sigmoid), δ → 0 exponentially with depth, (3) autograd (PyTorch, JAX) is reverse-mode automatic differentiation — same algorithm, automated via a computation graph.

sections.title: Vanishing Gradients & Residual Connections
sections.description:
Deeper networks outperform shallow ones — but training them is unstable. Gradients vanish (approach zero) or explode (diverge) with depth due to repeated matrix multiplication.

Vanishing gradient (sigmoid):  δᵣ = Πₗₜ₁ σ′(zₗ)Wₗ  → 0 as L grows   (every factor |W|·|σ′| < 1)
Exploding gradient (ReLU):    δᵣ = Πₗₜ₁ Wₗ           → ∞ as L grows   (|W| > 1 per layer)

Solution: Residual Connections (ResNet, He et al. 2015)
  Output:  y = F(x) + x           ← skip connection
  Gradient: ∂L/∂x = ∂L/∂y · (∂F/∂x + 1)  ← the +1 is a gradient highway

Even if ∂F/∂x → 0 (dead layers), the identity term carries the gradient backward. This enabled 152-layer ResNet vs VGG’s 19 layers.

FAANG insight: ResNet’s innovation is adding an identity bypass. This design pattern is universal today: every transformer block has residual connections, every diffusion model, every modern architecture. Always ask in interviews: “What prevents the gradient from vanishing at this depth?” If the answer isn’t “residual connections” or “normalization”, the architecture won’t scale.

sections.title: Normalization: Batch Norm vs Layer Norm vs RMS Norm
sections.description:
Normalization layers re-center and re-scale activations to stabilize training. Two dominant variants; FAANG interviews probe the difference.

Batch Normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015):
  y = γ·(x−μₓ)/σₓ + β      ← normalizes across batch dimension
  μₓ, σₓ: per-channel over the batch
  Pros: allows higher LR, slight regularization  |  Cons: batch-dependent, complex at inference

Layer Normalization (Ba et al., 2016):
  y = γ·(x−μℓ)/σℓ + β      ← normalizes across feature dimension
  μℓ, σℓ: per-sample over all features (batch-independent)
  Pros: works at batch size 1, deterministic at inference  |  Cons: fewer side benefits

RMS Norm (Zhang & Sennrich, 2019):
  y = γ·(x)/RMS(x)          ← removes re-centering (μ=0), simpler
  Used in: LLaMA, Gemma, Mistral — empirically equal to LayerNorm

FAANG insight: Why do transformers use LayerNorm not BatchNorm? Sequence models have variable-length inputs → batch statistics across different lengths are ill-defined. LayerNorm treats each token independently. The second critical insight is pre-norm vs post-norm: original Transformer used post-norm (norm after residual add); modern LLMs (GPT-3, LLaMA) use pre-norm (norm → sublayer → add). Pre-norm is more stable at depth because the gradient flows through the residual path without passing through the norm’s scaling factors.

[Intuition] Deep dive available: (→ see Neural Network Training §6 for BatchNorm vs LayerNorm comparison with TF code, dropout mask SVG, and FAANG Q&A)

sections.title: Optimization: SGD, Adam, AdamW & Warmup
sections.description:
Training neural networks requires carefully tuned optimizers. FAANG interviews test your understanding of why each component exists.

SGD:  wₜ₊₁ = wₜ − η·∂L/∂w           ← simple, generalizes well, but sensitive to LR
SGD+Momentum:  v = αv − η·g ; w += v  ← damped oscillation through ravines
Adam:  adaptive LR per-parameter using running mean of gradient & squared gradient
  m = β₁m + (1−β₁)g          ← estimate of 1st moment (mean)
  v = β₂v + (1−β₂)g²          ← estimate of 2nd moment (uncentered variance)
  w = w − η·m/(√v + ε)          ← normalized step (per-parameter)
AdamW:  decouples weight decay from gradient update (L₂ regularization via separate decay)
  w = w − η·(m/(√v+ε) + λw)  ← improves generalization vs Adam

Learning rate warmup (gradually increase LR from 0 to target over first N steps) prevents early divergence when the optimizer’s moment estimates are noisy. Standard values: β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e⁻⁸, weight decay λ=0.01–0.1.

FAANG insight: AdamW is the default for LLM training because it fixes Adam’s “L₂ regularization interacts badly with adaptive LRs” bug. Cosine LR schedule (decay to near-zero over training) is preferred over step decay for better convergence. Know the trade-off: SGD generalizes better on small data (< 100K examples), Adam/AdamW converges faster on large data.

[Intuition] Deep dive available: (→ see Neural Network Training §5, §8 for optimizer details and LR schedule deep dives with contour SVGs, TF code, and FAANG Q&A)

sections.title: 2012 — AlexNet, Dropout & the Deep Learning Revolution
sections.description:
[Concrete Numbers] Concrete numbers: AlexNet: 60M parameters, 650K neurons, 5 conv + 3 FC layers. Trained on 1.2M ImageNet images at 256×256 for 6 days on 2×GTX 580 (3GB each). Top-5 error: 15.3% (vs 26% runner-up). 1.1GB model storage. Dropout p=0.5 on FC layers; 2048× data augmentation via random crops/flips.

AlexNet (Krizhevsky, Sutskever & Hinton, 2012) crushed ImageNet — 15.3% top-5 error vs 26.2% for traditional CV — kickstarting the deep learning era.

Key innovations that carried forward:

  • ReLU activation: training was 6× faster than tanh equivalents
  • Dropout: randomly zero neurons during training (p=0.5) — prevents co-adaptation, acts as ensemble of sub-networks
  • Data augmentation: random crops, flips, color jitter — effectively infinite training data
  • GPU training: 2× GTX 580, model parallelism across GPUs
  • Local Response Normalization (LRN): lateral inhibition — later replaced by BatchNorm

FAANG insight: Dropout is a form of Bayesian approximation (Monte Carlo Dropout — Gal & Ghahramani, 2016): applying dropout at inference with multiple forward passes gives uncertainty estimates. It’s rarely used in modern LLMs (transformers prefer LayerNorm for regularization) but remains essential for smaller CNNs/MLPs. AlexNet’s real contribution was showing that depth + compute + data beats hand-engineered features — a thesis that still drives the field.

sections.title: 2017 — Transformers & the Attention Mechanism
sections.description:
[Concrete Numbers] Concrete numbers: The original Transformer: 65M params, 6 encoder + 6 decoder layers, 512 hidden dim, 8 attention heads (64 dim/head), 2048 FFN dim. Trained on WMT 2014 English-German (4.5M sentence pairs) for 12 hours on 8×P100 GPUs. BLEU score: 28.4 (vs 26.7 SOTA). Training cost: ~$50 on cloud GPUs. Modern LLMs: GPT-3 175B trained on 3.14E23 FLOPs, LLaMA 3 70B on 6.4M GPU-hours.

The Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) replaced recurrence with self-attention: each token attends to every other token in parallel. This parallelization unlocked training on “web-scale” data.

Scaled Dot-Product Attention:
  Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(Q·Kᵀ / √dₖ) · V
  Q = X·Wᴱ, K = X·Wᴰ, V = X·Wᴱ       ← learned projections from input X
  dₖ = head dimension (typically 64–128)  ← scaling prevents softmax saturation

Multi-Head Attention (h parallel heads, usually 8–96): each head learns different relationship types (syntax, semantics, position). Outputs are concatenated and projected.

FAANG insight: Self-attention is a set operation — it permutes with the input order. Positional encodings (sinusoidal, learned, RoPE) are required to inject sequence structure. Causal masking (triangular mask) prevents attending to future tokens. The KV cache stores K,V for all previous tokens at inference, trading memory for O(1) per-step compute. This is why LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound.

Interview question: Why scale by √dₖ? Without scaling, large d leads to large dot products → softmax gradients approach zero (saturated). Scaling keeps variance at ~1, preserving gradient flow.

sections.title: 2020s — Scaling Laws & Foundation Models
sections.description:
Kaplan et al. (2020) and Hoffmann et al. (2022) discovered power-law scaling: model performance improves predictably with more parameters, data, and compute — until one resource is exhausted.

Scaling Law (Kaplan et al., 2020):
  L(N,D) = a/Nᵃ + b/Dᵄ + c      ← loss = data term + model term + irreducible
  N: parameters, D: tokens, α≈0.076, β≈0.103 (power-law exponents)

Chinchilla Optimal (Hoffmann et al., 2022):
  The optimal allocation for a given compute budget C is:
  N_opt = kᵃ·Cᵂᵃ,  D_opt = kᴵ·Cᵂᵆ  ← roughly 20 tokens per parameter

This means most early LLMs (GPT-3: 175B params, 300B tokens) were undertrained — they should have used ~3.5T tokens. Chinchilla-optimal training (more data, fewer params) gives better performance for the same compute.

FAANG insight: Scaling laws explain why the industry shifted to massive data collection. They also predict diminishing returns — improving the data quality (e.g., deduplication, filtering) is now more impactful than adding more data. The bitter lesson (Sutton, 2019): general methods that scale with compute (search, learning) consistently beat hand-engineered approaches in the long run.

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A — Neural Network Evolution
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] FAANG Interview Questions

Q1: "Why did ReLU replace sigmoid/tanh as the default activation?" — Google L4

A: ReLU doesn't saturate (derivative = 1 for x > 0), so gradients flow freely through deep networks. Sigmoid's max derivative is 0.25 — for a 10-layer network, the best-case gradient is 0.25¹⁰ ≈ 10⁻⁶, effectively zero. ReLU is also computationally cheaper (just max(0,x) vs exp()). The trade-off is dead ReLU (neurons stuck at 0), which is mitigated by careful initialization (He init) and alternatives like Leaky ReLU or GELU.

Q2: "What problem did BatchNorm solve and how?" — Meta E4

A: BatchNorm solved the internal covariate shift problem: as earlier layers update during training, the distribution of inputs to later layers keeps shifting, making training unstable. BatchNorm normalizes each layer's inputs to have zero mean and unit variance (using batch statistics), then applies learnable scale γ and shift β. This allows higher learning rates, reduces sensitivity to initialization, and provides a slight regularization effect (because each batch has slightly different statistics).

Q3: "Why was AlexNet (2012) a breakthrough?" — Amazon L4

A: AlexNet was the first deep CNN to win ImageNet, dropping top-5 error from 26% to 16%. Three innovations: (1) ReLU activations instead of tanh — enabled deeper nets without vanishing gradients. (2) Dropout for regularization — prevented co-adaptation in the massive FC layers. (3) GPU training with data parallelism — showed that compute-scale was the path forward. It also used data augmentation (random crops, color jitter) effectively.

Q4: "Why did Transformers replace RNNs for sequence tasks?" — Google L5, OpenAI L5

A: Three fundamental limitations of RNNs: (1) Sequential computation prevents parallelization within a sequence (O(n) steps vs O(1) for Transformer). (2) Vanishing gradients over long sequences, even with LSTMs (the effective context length of LSTMs is ~200 tokens). (3) The hidden state is a fixed-size bottleneck — a single vector must encode all past information. Transformers solve all three: parallel self-attention (bidirectional context in O(1) steps), direct token-to-token paths (no gradient vanishing), and flexible context via attention weights.

Q5: "What do scaling laws tell us about model performance?" — OpenAI L5

A: Scaling laws (Kaplan et al., 2020) show a power-law relationship between loss and model size/data/compute: L(N,D) ∝ 1/Nᵃ + 1/Dᵐ⁽. This means throwing more parameters or data predictably improves performance — until one resource becomes the bottleneck. Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022) refined this: for optimal compute allocation, you need ~20 tokens per parameter. Most early LLMs were undertrained (GPT-3: 175B params, 300B tokens → should have been ~3.5T tokens). The bitter lesson: general methods that scale with compute consistently beat hand-engineered approaches.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• McCulloch, W. & Pitts, W. "A Logical Calculus of Ideas Immanent in Nervous Activity." — 1943
  • Rosenblatt, F. "The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage and Organization in the Brain." — 1958
  • Rumelhart, D., et al. "Learning representations by back-propagating errors." — 1986
  • Krizhevsky, A., et al. "ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks." (AlexNet) — 2012
  • Vaswani, A., et al. "Attention Is All You Need." (Transformer) — 2017
  • He, K., et al. "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition." (ResNet) — 2015
  • Ioffe, S. & Szegedy, C. "Batch Normalization." — 2015
  • Ba, J., et al. "Layer Normalization." — 2016
  • Kingma, D. & Ba, J. "Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization." — 2015
  • Kaplan, J., et al. "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models." — 2020
  • Hoffmann, J., et al. "Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models." (Chinchilla) — 2022
  • Sutton, R. "The Bitter Lesson." — 2019
